In-place composting around flower pots and allelopathy  Because of limited space for gardening, we have a fairly large number of trees and plants in flower pots. In particular I have found that citrus trees seem to demand a lot of mulch around them and so I am doing sheet mulch and in place composting in the pots. There is usually a 2-4 inch layer of leaf fall mulch covering a thin layer of kitchen waste in these pots. The leaf fall is currently mostly mango and papaya leaves. The mango leaves are usually green when put in, the papaya leaves may be harvested when the show that they are about to fade. I would assume most of this would be considered a "green" for composting purposes and that the soil and plant acts as the main nitrogen sink. Sometimes I add mango twigs as a brown, however.  I would also like to add that most pots contain between one and three different plant species. Examples include lemon and ginseng, eggplant and ginseng, some sort of sour starfruit relative, celery, and ginseng, eggplant, lime, and durian, and the so forth (yes I am growing a lot of ginseng-- I have almost 20 ginseng plants and counting, most of which are only a few months old).  My question is this. When composting above-ground parts of plants in an area where there is not much topsoil, such as a flower pot, to what extent do I need to worry about allelopathy between plant species? Is this a serious issue I need to keep a close eye on? Or is it less of an issue since typically I am not dealing with a lot of roots? Recycled and Used Fashion  After four years living in San Francisco, I'm really feeling the urge to update my wardrobe.  I've never been much of a fashionista, but that's partly because of the damage I assumed caused to the environment.  What are some websites or stores that offer either modern second hand clothes OR companies that take old clothing and turnit into something new?  How sustainable is recycled fashion? You hear that recycled aluminum actually has significant embodied process energy. Is process energy signficant for recycling clothes?  Also, any indication if how much water and land you are saving by purchasing recycled clothes (ie gains from re-using the textile materials)? There are several methods to treat sea water to make it suitable for household use, the primary goal being the removal of salt and other impurities. The two most common techniques are reverse osmosis and distillation. Reverse osmosis involves forcing sea water through a semi-permeable membrane to remove salts and contaminants, producing fresh water. Distillation involves heating sea water to create steam, which is then condensed into fresh water, leaving salts behind. While treating sea water for drinking or household purposes requires specialized equipment, untreated sea water can also be used directly for flushing toilets, irrigation of salt-tolerant plants, and certain cleaning

Aquaponics without water pumps: Is it possible?  We have a big sort of pond which is aerated by a largish aquarium pump (air pump only). We have carp and two turtles in the pond. My guess is the capacity is in the 1000 to 1500 gallon range.  I was wondering about the possibility of incorporating aquaponics into the structure as it currently exists. Ideas included adding styrofoam deep water floats with some light plants on them or trying to grow aquatic plants for food (lotus being a possible example). Given that the water does maintain ample aeration, what sorts of problems am I likely to face? Aquaponics without water pumps: Is it possible? In an aquaponics system that uses only air pumps for aeration and no water pumps, it's possible to grow plants, but there are some limitations and challenges to consider. Without water circulation from a pump, the distribution of nutrients to plant roots can be uneven, and plant beds farther from the source of fish waste may not receive enough nutrients. Floating rafts with lightweight plants such as lettuce or watercress can work if positioned where water movement (from bubbler action) is greatest. However, the system will be less efficient than pumped systems, and you may experience issues with solid waste buildup and limited

Then all the energy is used to build more mirrors and solar cells to extend the power plant's capacity. This way it's capacity can slowly but exponentially grow on free energy. There are no known large-scale projects that are fully self-replicating yet, but the concept you describe is related to 'bootstrapping' renewable energy systems and ideas like self-expanding solar farms. Some research projects and proposals have explored using energy from existing solar farms to manufacture additional solar panels, mirrors, or components to expand capacity. For example, some startup companies and academic projects have demonstrated using solar electricity to power small-scale manufacturing of solar panel components, and various proposals in the field of 'renewable-powered manufacturing' aim to close the loop even further. Some natural, eco-friendly alternatives to traditional air fresheners include placing bowls of baking soda or activated charcoal around the area to absorb odors, using coffee grounds in a muslin bag as a deodorizer, simmering citrus peels and spices like cinnamon on the stove, or adding essential oils to a diffuser or cotton balls. Houseplants can also help purify air naturally. These options avoid the chemicals and synthetic fragrances found in most commercial air fresheners, making them better for both health and the environment.

What are the environmental effects of discarding radioactive smoke detectors?  I called Kidde about mailing back their radioactive smoke detector now that it has exceeded its usable life. It is labeled that it contains 0.9 micro curies of Americium 241. They claimed over the telephone that the device is safe to discard in the trash.  I was hoping to learn exactly what are the environmental impacts of doing so? I am under the impression that the device will be compressed during the trip to the landfill, destroying the protective casing for the material. I am under the impression that the device will be compressed during the trip to the landfill, destroying the protective casing for the material. When smoke detectors containing Americium-241 are disposed of in landfills, the Am-241 is usually sealed in a small chamber or disc within the detector. If the detector is crushed or broken, the protective casing may be compromised, potentially allowing a tiny amount of radioactive material to be released. However, because the amount of Americium-241 is extremely small (0.9 microcuries), its potential for environmental contamination is low, especially when dispersed in a large landfill. Nonetheless, accumulation of large numbers of detectors could increase risk, and the radioactive material can persist in the environment. For

Sustainable methods of mosquito control  Right now I am in Jakarta (planning on returning to the US sooner or later) and of course mosquitos are a problem over here.  The common method over here to control mosquitos is to spray airborne insecticides frequently to kill mosquitos in the house. We do what we can, with a single small fan-driven indoor bugzapper but given that all electricity here is coal-fired would like to try to minimize this as well. I have tried using dried zodia leaves which seem to be mildly effective, and we keep fish and turtles in a pond out back. Is there anything more we can do to reduce the chemical and electrical load of this? For non-chemical, low-energy mosquito control indoors and outdoors in tropical climates, several effective methods are recommended: 1) Use physical barriers such as window and door screens, and bed nets, especially at night. 2) Eliminate standing water around your home to remove mosquito breeding sites. Check gutters, plant trays, and any other items that can collect water. 3) Use natural repellents such as citronella, lemongrass, or zodia plants around living spaces. 4) Encourage natural predators like fish (which you already have), dragonflies, and birds. 5

My family and I enjoy using it purely for recreation. I’ve been trying to limit my carbon footprint, and in doing so, wondered how much a fire in the fire pit contributes to it. We usually have about 2 to 3 logs, and a handful of sticks. So what’s an estimate for how many pounds of CO2 are released every hour of from an average sized camp fire? Are there other gases released other than CO2 that are harmful to the environment? Burning wood in a fire pit releases carbon dioxide (CO2) as a result of combustion. On average, burning one pound of dry wood releases about 1.5 to 1.8 pounds of CO2. If you burn 2 to 3 average-sized logs (about 10–15 pounds of wood total) over an hour, this would emit roughly 15–27 pounds of CO2 per hour. But I'd prefer not getting turmeric everywhere, and since I can't exactly find out where the ants are coming from, it wouldn't be as effective anyways. Besides turmeric, several natural or non-toxic household ingredients can help repel ants in the kitchen. Some effective options include: vinegar (especially white vinegar, which can be diluted with water and sprayed on surfaces), cinnamon (both ground and in oil form), lemon juice, and diatomaceous earth (food-grade). These can be applied around entry points, along ant trails, and in areas where ants are commonly seen. All are safe for use in kitchens, but be mindful to keep food-grade diatomaceous earth away from direct food contact. Regular cleaning to remove food traces will also help deter ants.

Electricity need to switch all cars from fossil fuel  For purely hypothetical discussion, I was asking myself how big electrical production increase would be needed for a first-world country when switching all cars from fossil fuel to EV only. I made the calculation myself for the country I live in (Italy, though the reasoning in principle would be valid for any chosen country), but the result is somehow unconvincing to me. So I would like to share my reasoning here and read you comments about it. Please note this is a very rough estimation, so please point out lack of precision in the calculation only when really matters to the general result, keeping in mind this is a very high level calculation.  In the examples I will reference data from 2019 which are the most recent complete dataset I was able to find.  The annual consumption of fuel for transportation was 7.3 million metric tons in gasoline and 23.8 million metric tons in diesel. The energy content is 46.9 MJ/kg for gasoline and 45.8 MJ/kg for diesel. The energy content sums up to about 344 PJ and 1090 PJ respectively. Converting to Wh (for easy comparison with electric consumption data) the total is just short of 400 TWh.  I found out that the efficiency of an internal combustion engine mounted on a vehicle is something around 20% - 35%, so I assumed an average value of 30% for the sake of simplicity. So the real energy that goes to motion is around 120 TWh. The efficiency of a full electric vehicle seems to me to be around 75%, so the electricity needed to charge that hypothetical EV fleet would be a little less than 160 TWh.  In the same year 2019, the electricity consumption in Italy was 320 TWh. So the conclusion of all this reasoning is that a country should roughly add 50% of its electrical production to support a full transition to electrical mobility.  Instinctively this seems way too much: have I made any serious error in my assumptions or calculations? Or is this basically a correct conclusion? Why don't they make playgrounds generate electricity?  Why don't they make playgrounds generate electricity (at least, not on a large scale)? It's a pity that all that energy of children horsing around goes to waste instead of being harnessed. You could incorporate a generator into a swing, for example. Why don't they make playgrounds generate electricity?  Why don't they make playgrounds generate electricity (at least, not on a large scale)? It's a pity that all that energy of children horsing around goes to waste instead of being harnessed. You could incorporate a generator into a swing, for example. Why don't they make playgrounds generate electricity?  Why don't they make playgrounds generate electricity (at least, not on a large scale)? It's a pity that all that energy of children horsing around goes to waste instead of being harnessed. You could incorporate a generator into a swing, for example. The most effective playground equipment for generating electricity would be those that involve repetitive motion or activities that engage significant physical movement, such as swings, see-saws, or merry-go-rounds. However, the main reason playgrounds aren't widely used to generate electricity is that the amount of energy produced by children's play is relatively small compared to the electrical needs of a community or even a building. Additionally, the cost and complexity of installing and maintaining such systems may outweigh the benefits. There are also safety considerations to ensure that integrating generators does not pose risks to children.

How would a garden adapted to drought look like?  Hypothetical question, since I neither have a garden, nor live in a region seriously affected by drought (yet).  In context of the current drought along the western US coast, experts and scientists ask citizens to save water, e.g. by not watering the gras, or even completely removing it (source: last paragraph of this german news article https://www.tagesschau.de/ausland/feuer-oregon-101.html).  This left me wondering, how such a garden could look like, especially since vegetation has a cooling effect. I am thinking about a few (fruit) trees, maybe some pots with plants adapted to dry/hot climate, some low bushes and otherwise mostly bare dirt, e.g. like the mediterranean Garrigue.  Are there any concept studies out there, or maybe even real examples? How would a garden adapted to drought look like? Are there any concept studies out there, or maybe even real examples? A drought-adapted garden typically features plants that require minimal watering and are well-suited to hot, dry conditions. Suitable plants include succulents (such as agave and sedum), Mediterranean herbs (like lavender, rosemary, and thyme), native grasses, and some drought-resistant shrubs and trees. The layout often uses mulch or gravel instead of grass lawns to help retain soil moisture and reduce water evaporation. Such gardens may also include features like rainwater harvesting systems or permeable surfaces. There are real-world examples, such as xeriscaped gardens in California and the southwestern US, that demonstrate these principles

Determining carbon reduction vs cost of various home upgrades  I've done some amount of upgrades to my house to reduce my overall carbon emissions, and reading online there are all kinds of suggestions for doing even more:  Replace my natural gas water heater with electric Put solar panels on the roof Buy wind energy credits Other kinds of carbon offsets Geothermal Don't replace my electric oven with gas (which I had been thinking about, since I hear how great they are for cooking) And of course I only have a limited amount of money to spend on this, so I'm trying to determine which action gives me the best bang (most carbon reduced) for my buck.  But I almost immediately run into a problem. Even just looking at that first one - "Replace my natural gas water heater with electric" - I can easily determine what the cost would be, but figuring out the carbon impact of that is tricky. I live in Colorado which is moving towards wind power, but still has some coal plants. How much carbon is my natural gas water heater emitting now? How much carbon would an electric water heater emit (when you take into account the carbon emitted to generate the electricity)?  Another example - my house was built in the 1970s so it doesn't have a lot of the energy-saving features built in to more recent houses, and I had an energy audit done that identified a lot of small items (nothing big). For example, where the tops of my walls meet the ceiling joists there are lots of tiny air gaps. Sealing those would be quite expensive, but might be worth it depending on how much energy I would save.  Is there a resource (or resources) that would help me figure out how much carbon would be reduced by each possible action? Please note that I'm not looking for common knowledge or general lists, I'm really trying to drill down and determine for my specific house, what are the exact (or approximate) impacts of each possible action? I realize that answering that question for each action will require specific information about my house and situation, I'm hoping that somewhere online there are calculators that would help me understand the impact of each action. Will using a less white paper (low CIE value) usually be more sustainable? Producing whiter copy paper generally requires more intensive bleaching processes, which involve increased use of chemicals and energy. Papers with a lower CIE value (less white, more natural or greyish) are often less heavily processed, use fewer chemicals, and may include a higher proportion of recycled content. Therefore, using less white paper is usually more sustainable, as it can reduce the environmental impact associated with chemical bleaching and energy consumption.

How much additional CO2 emissions will result from recent changes to US vehicle regulations?  From the Guardian, March 31, 2020: Trump to roll back Obama-era clean car rules in huge blow to climate fight (emphasis added):  The Trump administration is rolling back the US government’s strongest attempt to combat the climate crisis, weakening rules which compel auto companies to produce more fuel-efficient vehicles. [...] The changes to Obama-era regulations will allow vehicles to emit about a billion more tons of heat-trapping carbon dioxide – equivalent to roughly a fifth of annual US emissions.  Some are claiming that this change will directly result in the full billion tons of added emissions. But just because the requirement has changed, does not mean that automakers will automatically change the mileage or emissions controls on the vehicles they are producing. This is confirmed later in the article (emphasis added):  The Obama administration required auto companies to make vehicles 4.7% more efficient each year. The Trump administration initially wanted to freeze any progress on fuel efficiency past 2020. But its final rule, written by the EPA and the Department of Transportation, sets an improvement rate of 1.5% per year – or an industry average of 40.4 miles per gallon by 2026. That’s far less than the 2.4% per year by which the industry has said it will increase standards without any regulation.  Is there data or estimates on what the actual increase in emissions will be as a result of the new rule? Apparently the auto industry is capable of exceeding the current standard with no regulatory pressure, but will it now move the opposite way? Burning plastic at home, even in a small-scale incinerator, is not recommended due to the release of toxic gases (such as dioxins and furans) and incomplete combustion, which can be hazardous to your health and the environment. There is limited credible research or products supporting home-scale plastic incinerators precisely because of these risks. Most scientific literature and environmental agencies recommend against homemade or small-scale incineration, advocating instead for recycling, reduction, or participation in community or municipal waste management programs. The safest method to dispose of leftover durable water repellent spray containing PFAS is to take the can to a hazardous waste drop-off location in your area, such as Toronto's Household Hazardous Waste Depot. Do not pour out or use up the product if you wish to minimize environmental impact, and do not give it away to simply shift the environmental risk. While standard municipal incineration may not completely destroy PFAS, specialized hazardous waste management routes are the best currently available option to reduce harm.

What is the difference between biodegradable and compostable plastic?  I read this and still don't fully understand the difference. Which decomposes faster? Do they mean biodegradables don't need "certain" conditions so they are good to go into a bin (or a hole in your backyard)? Or do they mean biodegradable plastic leave some non-soil residue behind?  Plastic that is compostable is biodegradable, but not every plastic that is biodegradable is compostable. Whereas biodegradable plastic may be engineered to biodegrade in soil or water, compostable plastic refers to biodegradation into soil conditioning material (i.e., compost) under a certain set of conditions. In order for a plastic to be labeled as commercially “compostable” it must able to be broken down by biological treatment at a commercial or industrial composting facility. Composting utilizes microorganisms, heat and humidity to yield carbon dioxide, water, inorganic compounds, and biomass that is similar in characteristic to the rest of the finished compost product. Decomposition of the plastic must occur at a rate similar to the other elements of the material being composted (within 6 months) and leave no toxic residue that would adversely impact the ability of the finished compost to support plant growth. ASTM Standards D6400 and D6868 outline the specifications that must be met in order to label a plastic as commercially “compostable”. There are currently no ASTM standard test methods in place for evaluating the ability of a plastic to compost in a home environment. Setting a hot water cylinder thermostat below 60°C increases the risk of bacterial growth, particularly Legionella pneumophila, which can cause Legionnaires' disease—a serious kind of pneumonia. Therefore, the recommendation to keep the temperature at or above 60°C is a valid health and safety concern, not just a disclaimer. Maintaining at least 60°C in the cylinder is important even if the water isn't for drinking, since Legionella can be inhaled as aerosolized droplets from taps or showers.

For mild winter temperatures (rarely below -5°C at night), one of the most economical and convenient inner insulation options is rigid foam board insulation (such as polystyrene or PIR boards). These are cost-effective, offer high insulation (high R-value per inch), and are resistant to moisture and mold when installed with proper vapor barriers. They are also easy to install on existing walls and are robust against everyday impacts. Adding a layer of plasterboard on top can further improve durability and finish. Mineral wool (like Rockwool) is another healthy option: it's non-combustible, good for sound insulation

Do horizontal top-loaded washing machines shed more fibers from clothes than front-loaded ones?  I read that clothes shed more fibers in top-loaded washing machines than in front-loaded ones. Does that also apply to horizontal top-loaded ones? Do horizontal top-loaded washing machines shed more fibers from clothes than front-loaded ones?  I read that clothes shed more fibers in top-loaded washing machines than in front-loaded ones. Does that also apply to horizontal top-loaded ones? Do horizontal top-loaded washing machines shed more fibers from clothes than front-loaded ones?  I read that clothes shed more fibers in top-loaded washing machines than in front-loaded ones. Does that also apply to horizontal top-loaded ones? The main factor influencing fiber shedding is the washing action. Traditional top-loading washing machines with a central agitator (vertical-axis) generally cause more fiber shedding than front-loading machines (horizontal-axis) due to their rougher action. However, horizontal-axis top-loading machines (which function similarly to front-loaders but have a lid on top) are gentler on clothes and generally shed fewer fibers—comparable to front-loaders. So, the higher fiber shedding is mostly associated with vertical-axis top-loaders, not horizontal-axis (front-load or horizontal top-load) machines.

Why are there no energy star certifications for portable fans?  From what I understand no portable electric heaters have the certification because using a portable heater is not efficient at all.  Ceiling fans and ventilation fans are certified; however, portable fans are not. Is there a reasoning behind this? Energy Star certification is awarded to products that meet strict energy efficiency guidelines set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) or the Department of Energy (DOE). Currently, portable or box fans are not eligible for Energy Star certification because the EPA has determined that the potential for energy savings with these products is limited. Unlike ceiling fans or ventilation fans, portable fans generally use relatively little energy, and there is less variation in the energy efficiency among models. As a result, creating meaningful efficiency standards and a certification program for portable fans has not been a priority.

The European Union's Real Driving Emissions (RDE) tests are designed to measure the pollutant emissions of vehicles under real-world driving conditions, using portable emissions measurement systems (PEMS) while the vehicle is driven on public roads. The RDE procedure attempts to represent a wide range of driving situations, including various speeds, accelerations, road types, and conditions such as urban, rural, and motorway driving. Incinerating plastic waste and sending it to landfill sites both have significant environmental consequences, but they differ in their impacts. Incineration of plastics releases greenhouse gases (like CO2), toxic chemicals, and air pollutants such as dioxins and furans, which can be harmful to both the environment and human health. Even with modern incinerators, some emissions still escape controls. On the other hand, discarding plastics in landfills avoids these air pollutants but leads to long-term environmental issues: plastics persist for hundreds of years, can leach harmful chemicals into groundwater, and contribute to soil and water

Environmentally safe methods for recycling or disposing of ceramics with unknown glazes or finishes involve several steps. First, if the ceramics aren’t reusable, contact your local waste management facility to see if they offer specialized disposal options, as most curbside recycling does not accept ceramics. The reason is that glazes, especially older or imported pottery, may contain hazardous elements like lead or cadmium. Lead test kits (available at hardware stores) can help determine if a glaze contains lead, but professional analysis may be needed for accuracy. If you can't confirm the safety of the glaze, do not add broken ceramics to compost

Are foam netting sleeves recyclable?  I am wondering if foam netting sleeves that are often used to protect glass bottles in transport (see picture) can be recycled in a soft plastic stream? Are foam netting sleeves recyclable?  I am wondering if foam netting sleeves that are often used to protect glass bottles in transport (see picture) can be recycled in a soft plastic stream? Are foam netting sleeves recyclable?  I am wondering if foam netting sleeves that are often used to protect glass bottles in transport (see picture) can be recycled in a soft plastic stream? Foam netting sleeves are typically made from expanded polyethylene (EPE) or sometimes expanded polystyrene (EPS). EPE is a type of #4 plastic (LDPE), while EPS is #6 (polystyrene). Technically, both materials are recyclable, but their acceptance depends on your local recycling program. EPE foam sleeves are sometimes accepted in soft plastic recycling streams, but you should check with your local recycling facility to confirm. EPS sleeves are less commonly recycled. Always check local guidelines to ensure proper disposal.

When food waste is sent to a landfill instead of being composted, the environmental impacts are significantly different. In landfills, food and other organic waste decompose anaerobically (without oxygen), which produces methane, a potent greenhouse gas that contributes to climate change. Composting, however, allows organic waste to break down in the presence of oxygen, producing far less methane and resulting in a valuable soil amendment instead of greenhouse gases. While compostable materials are not toxic like plastics, sending them to landfill still causes environmental harm because of the methane emissions. The cooling is much easier when there is a lot of cool water available, so most data centers are built in countries with a cool climate.  But at the same time, the countries with a cool climate consume a lot of energy to warm up water for their heatings.  Why does Microsoft experiment with a data center under sea, instead of using the data center's warm water to heat surrounding buildings? There are similar solutions in other areas, e.g. combined heat and power facilities with district heating schemes.  I've found some articles about data centers involved in district heating, but I'm wondering why this isn't done more often. Are there any obstacles, hidden costs, or other problems (like, keeping the location of the data center a secret)? Capturing and transporting waste heat from data centers to nearby households and offices involves several technical challenges. First, the temperature of waste heat from data centers is typically lower (often 30–50°C) than traditional heat sources, which can make it less effective for heating without additional equipment such as heat pumps. Setting up infrastructure—like insulated pipes and heat exchangers—to connect data centers to district heating systems can be expensive and logistically challenging, especially in densely populated urban areas or where distances are great. What is the environmental impact of meal kit subscriptions (Blue Apron, etc)?  According to Wikipedia, a meal-kit is...  ...a subscription service that sends customers pre-portioned food ingredients and recipes for them to prepare homecooked meals.  Well-known examples include Blue Apron, Hello Fresh, and Green Chef. These kits include single meal portions of meats, produce, and seasonings, typically assuming that customers will only have at home such basics as oil, salt, and water.  One claimed advantage to such services is reduced food waste -- each kit contains the exact amount of all perishable ingredients needed for the recipe, meaning there's no excess produce to spoil once the meal is made.  However, the entire thing requires boxes, ice packs, and plastic packaging -- not all of which is recyclable.  For now, let's ignore the question of home delivery vs going to stores. My question is:  Do meal-kits reduce total food waste? Do meal-kits increase the amount of packaging waste produced per meal? Research comparing meal kit delivery services and traditional grocery shopping has found that meal kits can reduce total food waste, as they provide pre-portioned ingredients, decreasing the likelihood of unused perishables being thrown out. However, meal kits typically generate more packaging waste per meal, due to the need to individually wrap ingredients and keep them fresh during delivery. While the reduction in food waste does contribute positively to environmental impact, studies suggest that the increased packaging—much of which is not recyclable—can offset or even outweigh those benefits. Laptop nights: shutdown or sleep?  Assuming I use it all day long, should I shutdown my (recent) laptop every night and power it up every morning, or just let it sleep by closing it? Laptop nights: shutdown or sleep?  Assuming I use it all day long, should I shutdown my (recent) laptop every night and power it up every morning, or just let it sleep by closing it? Laptop nights: shutdown or sleep?  Assuming I use it all day long, should I shutdown my (recent) laptop every night and power it up every morning, or just let it sleep by closing it? If you use your (recent) laptop all day long, both shutting down and letting it sleep overnight are viable options, but there are trade-offs. Sleep mode allows you to resume work quickly and uses very little power, but it still draws some battery over time. Shutdown saves power completely and clears temporary system files, which can help maintain system performance. Modern laptops are designed to handle frequent sleep cycles without harm, so you can safely leave it in sleep mode for a few nights. However, it's a good idea to fully shut down or restart your laptop every few days to install updates and refresh the system.

Does responsible living leave more space to unresponsible living?  It looks to me that the efforts I'm doing to make my living more sustainable are worthless.  Indeed, when some of us make efforts to lower energy consumption, doesn't it only leave harvestable resources for the others at lower price (because of market law "offer & demand" making prices)?  If too many others have no clue how harmful they are for future generation, and only consume more because it is cheaper, is there any point making any effort in the direction of sustainability? Does responsible living leave more space to unresponsible living?  It looks to me that the efforts I'm doing to make my living more sustainable are worthless.  Indeed, when some of us make efforts to lower energy consumption, doesn't it only leave harvestable resources for the others at lower price (because of market law "offer & demand" making prices)?  If too many others have no clue how harmful they are for future generation, and only consume more because it is cheaper, is there any point making any effort in the direction of sustainability? This is a classic question in environmental economics, often referred to as the rebound or 'leakage' effect. When individuals reduce their consumption (for example, of energy), it can lead to lower demand and thus lower prices, making it more attractive for others to consume more. However, while this effect can offset some benefits of individual action, it does not make your efforts entirely worthless. Collective action, policy changes, and systemic shifts are also crucial in reducing overall consumption and preventing the rebound effect. Individual sustainable choices still contribute to raising awareness, influencing culture, and supporting broader policy efforts towards sustainability.

How environmentally friendly is palm oil with a certified organic label?  I'm trying to reduce my consumption of palm oil because palm oil production is responsible for deforestation, large forest fires and loss of biodiversity. The problem is that palm oil is present in many food and non-food products under many different names and it's sometimes difficult to find a good alternative.  When I can't find an alternative product without palm oil, I try to find one with a Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil Standard (RSPO) label because I know it's somewhat better than non-certified products. Even if RSPO standards are low it's probably the best label there is. It's more strict than the Indonesian Sustainable Palm Oil Standard (ISPO) and the Malaysian Palm Oil Standard (MSPO), at least according to this comparison. Unfortunately products with a RSPO label are scarce where I live (The Netherlands / Europe), but there are quite a lot of products with certified organic palm oil.  My question is: how environmentally friendly is organic palm oil, certified according to EU regulations? How does it compare to RSPO-certified oil and to products without any label? Do those providers, for instance, invest less in oil and coal projects? Or are they in any way more local or socially just regarding their overall business practices? What is a sustainable credit card?  How is a credit card marketed as "sustainable credit card" different from a conventional credit card?  Do those providers, for instance, invest less in oil and coal projects? Or are they in any way more local or socially just regarding their overall business practices? A sustainable credit card is typically issued by a financial institution that incorporates environmental, social, or ethical considerations into its business practices. These cards may be marketed as 'sustainable' if the provider avoids investing in fossil fuel industries like oil and coal, supports renewable energy, or funds projects with social and environmental benefits. Some issuers make additional efforts such as using recycled materials for the physical cards, contributing to climate-positive initiatives (like planting trees per transaction), or committing to greater transparency and local investment. In contrast, conventional credit card providers may have no specific focus on sustainability or ethical investment.

Are building certificates under EU Directive 2002/91/EC, such as EPC, Energieausweis, DPE, directly comparable between EU countries?  Implementing Directive 2002/91/EC, the UK have the Energy Performance Certificate (EPC), Belgium has the Energieprestatiecertificaat (EPC), Germany has Energieausweis, France has Diagnostic de performance énergétique (DPE), etc. All are graded A-G. Can they be directly compared?  For example, I rent a studio in England with an EPC which states a typical consumption of 354 kWh/m²/yr and classifies it as C. But on a Belgian real estate site I find homes with 205 kWh/m²/yr and D, 140 kwH/m²/yr and C, or 280 kWh/m²/yr and F, and the French Wikipedia would place 354 kWh/m²/yr in grade F. This Swedish certificate describes that new housing in Sweden must use at most 50 kWh/m²/yr, which there is apparently class C, even though Sweden is much colder than Belgium, France, or the UK.  I imagine it's not entirely fair to compare absolute heating costs between different climates. Is there a common system that defines the A-G scale?  Article 7  Energy performance certificate  Member States shall ensure that, when buildings are constructed, sold or rented out, an energy performance certificate is made available to the owner or by the owner to the prospective buyer or tenant, as the case might be. The validity of the certificate shall not exceed 10 years. Certification for apartments or units designed for separate use in blocks may be based:  on a common certification of the whole building for blocks with a common heating system, or  on the assessment of another representative apartment in the same block.  Member States may exclude the categories referred to in Article 4(3) from the application of this paragraph.  The energy performance certificate for buildings shall include reference values such as current legal standards and benchmarks in order to make it possible for consumers to compare and assess the energy performance of the building. The certificate shall be accompanied by recommendations for the cost-effective improvement of the energy performance. The objective of the certificates shall be limited to the provision of information and any effects of these certificates in terms of legal proceedings or otherwise shall be decided in accordance with national rules.  Member States shall take measures to ensure that for buildings with a total useful floor area over 1000 m2 occupied by public authorities and by institutions providing public services to a large number of persons and therefore frequently visited by these persons an energy certificate, not older than 10 years, is placed in a prominent place clearly visible to the public. The range of recommended and current indoor temperatures and, when appropriate, other relevant climatic factors may also be clearly displayed. Can pumped water energy storage be combined with rainwater harvesting? A recent question on what to do with harvested rainwater got me thinking about pumped energy storage systems. Since both systems require storage of water for later use, is there a cost-effective way in which the two could be combined? Specifically in small-scale applications (farms, rural communities, campuses) where rooftops are already available for rainwater harvesting.  As background, pumped energy storage involves pumping water to an elevated storage reservoir when energy is cheap and plentiful (excess wind, low demand, unused solar, etc) then letting it run through a turbine to generate power at times of low supply.  Some ideas about how this might work:  Rain water caught on the roofs of tall and/or elevated buildings could flow through a turbine (generating power) to shorter/lower reservoirs where it could be used for irrigation or other purposes Overhead water tanks could be fitted with solar panels to drive pumps bringing up rain water from lower rooftops, where it would later be flowed through a turbine to provide power A reservoir or tank could be built near a community or farm requiring water. Wind or solar power could pump water into the tank. When water is needed it could be flowed through a turbine Have any such systems been implemented? Could it be cost-effective, or would it simply over-complicate things? Can pumped water energy storage be combined with rainwater harvesting? Since both systems require storage of water for later use, is there a cost-effective way in which the two could be combined? Specifically in small-scale applications (farms, rural communities, campuses) where rooftops are already available for rainwater harvesting.  As background, pumped energy storage involves pumping water to an elevated storage reservoir when energy is cheap and plentiful (excess wind, low demand, unused solar, etc) then letting it run through a turbine to generate power at times of low supply.  Some ideas about how this might work:  Rain water caught on the roofs of tall and/or elevated buildings could flow through a turbine (generating power) to shorter/lower reservoirs where it could be used for irrigation or other purposes Overhead water tanks could be fitted with solar panels to drive pumps bringing up rain water from lower rooftops, where it would later be flowed through a turbine to provide power A reservoir or tank could be built near a community or farm requiring water. Wind or solar power could pump water into the tank. When water is needed it could be flowed through a turbine Have any such systems been implemented? Could it be cost-effective, or would it simply over-complicate things? Yes, pumped water energy storage can be combined with rainwater harvesting, especially for small-scale applications like farms, rural communities, or campuses. In such a setup, rainwater collected from rooftops or catchment areas could be stored in an elevated reservoir or tank. When excess renewable energy (from solar or wind) is available, it can be used to pump this stored water to a higher elevation. When energy is needed, water can be released through a micro-hydro turbine to generate electricity, after which the water can be used for irrigation or domestic use. Toilet paper is designed to break down rapidly in water once flushed. At a water treatment plant, the paper undergoes physical and biological processes. Initially, it is mechanically separated from the water, and most of it settles out as part of the sludge. In the following treatment stages, microorganisms decompose much of the organic matter, including the cellulose from toilet paper. During decomposition, carbon dioxide (CO2) and, in some cases, methane (CH4) are released, depending on whether the process is aerobic or anaerobic. Is it because of the scarce economy of scale in the current market? The high cost of hemp clothing compared to cotton is influenced by several main factors: Limited production scale—hemp is produced at a much smaller scale than cotton, which leads to higher raw material costs due to a lack of economies of scale. Additionally, processing infrastructure for hemp is less developed, resulting in higher manufacturing costs. These factors together make hemp clothing more expensive despite hemp being easy and sustainable to grow.

With community solar, you buy or lease a share in a solar project, often located out-of-state, and receive credits on your electricity bill or a share of the earnings from the energy produced. Companies such as Arcadia, Solarize, or other community solar programs allow participation without physically installing panels on your own roof. Most modern printer and pen inks are generally considered safe for composting in small quantities, as they are usually soy- or water-based and contain minimal heavy metals. Shredded white office paper can be a good source of 'brown' (carbon-rich) material for your compost, helping to balance out wet, nitrogen-rich materials. Add the shredded paper gradually and mix it well to improve aeration and reduce excess moisture. However, avoid glossy, heavily colored, or metallic papers, which may have coatings or inks that are less safe for composting.

The pots are in a metallic structure above the plates, so I can take the plates to reuse the water (throwing it at the plants again).  This reuse seems beneficial, because I think I can get rid of mosquitoes that would reproduce in the stagnated water. And also some nutrients of the soil (as well as earthworms) can return to the vase.  Is there some negative points in doing that? Yes, there are risks associated with spreading plant diseases or pests. When you reuse the runoff water from plant pots, it may contain pathogens, fungi, or pests from the soil which could be reintroduced to your plants or spread among different plants. In addition, reused runoff water may also accumulate excess salts or fertilizers, which could harm plant roots over time. To minimize these risks, ensure your plants are healthy and avoid reusing water if you notice signs of disease or pest infestations. Occasionally flushing the soil with fresh water can help prevent buildup of salts or chemicals.

How environmentally friendly is the production process of these vitamin B12 pills? Might it maybe even be better for the environment to occasionally eat meat instead of making these pills? The environmental impacts of producing vitamin B12 supplements are generally much lower than the impacts of raising livestock for meat. Vitamin B12 for supplements is typically produced through fermentation processes using specific microorganisms (such as certain bacteria). This method requires fewer resources (land, water, and energy) and results in lower greenhouse gas emissions compared to meat production, which involves feeding, raising, and processing animals. Therefore, taking vitamin B12 supplements is usually more environmentally friendly than obtaining B12 by eating meat.

I have already been using tea tree oil as a supplement to my conventional toothpaste. Do you consider it health and safe to only rely on tea tree oil to brush my teeth? Tea tree oil does have antibacterial properties, but it is not recommended to use it as the sole method for brushing teeth. Tea tree oil lacks fluoride, which is essential for preventing cavities and strengthening enamel. Additionally, using tea tree oil alone can cause mouth irritation, possible allergic reactions, and, if swallowed, even toxicity. Most dental associations do not approve tea tree oil as a primary oral hygiene product. For optimal dental health, it is better to use a fluoride-containing toothpaste and seek out toothpaste options with reduced or recyclable packaging if you are concerned about plastic waste.

At what time could we produce biochar for industrial application? Isn't it nonsense to even think of using biochar to trap carbon, when we're still using coal power plants? What's the applicability of biochar?  At what time could we produce biochar for industrial application? Isn't it nonsense to even think of using biochar to trap carbon, when we're still using coal power plants? Biochar is primarily used in agriculture to improve soil fertility, retain soil moisture, and reduce nutrient leaching. In industrial applications, biochar can be used for water filtration, as a component in building materials, and for carbon sequestration. Industrial-scale biochar production is already possible using existing pyrolysis technologies, and facilities are in operation worldwide. While it is true that using biochar to trap carbon will have a limited effect if emissions from coal power plants continue at high rates, biochar can still play a role in carbon management and sustainable agriculture as part of a broader climate strategy.

I don't want to bind the reservoir, panel and tap locations to the natural circulation cycle though (hot water traveling up etc).  So, in order for this to work, I'd have to force some very slow water circulation between the reservoir and the panels; a pump that takes very little power and provides very small but constant throughput: something with power requirements low enough to make this economically viable, maintenance-proof and preferably silent. For low-flow solar hot water circulation, the most energy-efficient pumps are typically DC-powered brushless circulator pumps, known for low power consumption and long lifespan. Notable examples include the Laing D5 or similar small circulators, which can run directly from a small solar panel. These pumps are maintenance-free, very quiet, and are designed for potable (drinking-quality) water. Throughput can be precisely controlled, and their power draw is sufficiently low to make operation economically viable in most installations. Look for models specifically designed for solar water heating systems to ensure compatibility with low-pressure, clean water applications.

To sustainably grow asparagus and reduce water use, you can plant ground covers or suitable companion plants between and around asparagus to cover the soil, reduce evaporation, suppress weeds, and provide some nutrient benefits. Good companion plants for asparagus include: parsley, basil, nasturtiums, marigold, and spinach. Annual crops like lettuce, beets, or even strawberries are often recommended for interplanting since they grow low to the ground and help shade the soil, keeping it moist and minimizing weeds. The environmental impacts of plastics depend heavily on the type of plastic and its lifecycle. For backyard or DIY projects with a focus on sustainability, it's best to avoid single-use plastics like polystyrene (PS, #6) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC, #3) due to their toxicity and difficulty to recycle. Prefer plastics that are more easily recycled, such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET, #1) and high-density polyethylene (HDPE, #2). Look for post-consumer recycled (PCR) plastics where possible, and consider bioplastics or compostable materials when suitable.

Rotational grazing, limiting stocking density, and carefully timing grazing periods can help preserve forest health. Some affordable materials and methods for repairing rain-damaged roads made of Damara (a locally available soil or gravel) include using gravel or crushed stone as an overlay to fill in potholes and ruts. Applying cold mix asphalt patches to damaged areas can also help create a more water-resistant surface. Additionally, improving drainage alongside the road—such as by digging ditches or adding culverts—can help prevent water from collecting and causing further damage. Regular maintenance, like re-grading and compacting the surface after heavy rains, will also extend the road's life at minimal cost.

Not all components of a tea bag are necessarily biodegradable or safe to compost. The 'bag' itself is usually made of paper and is compostable, but the thread and staple can be more problematic. Most cotton threads are compostable, but some can contain synthetic fibers which are not. The staple is metal and will not break down but instead corrode over time. For best composting practice, remove the staple before composting the rest of the tea bag, including the thread if you're certain it's cotton or another natural fiber. The fuel efficiency of car-sized road-rail vehicles (often known as Hi-Rail vehicles) operating on train tracks is generally better than driving on roads because steel wheels on steel rails have less rolling resistance than rubber tires on pavement. For a typical pickup truck outfitted as a Hi-Rail vehicle, fuel consumption while operating on rails can be around 8–10 liters per 100 kilometers (about 24–29 mpg), depending on factors like speed, track quality, and vehicle load—this is often slightly more efficient than the same vehicle driving on roads. How to recognize products with neonicotinoid pesticides in them?  Recently, the EU has temporarily banned neonicotinoid pesticides because there are strong indications that these pesticides are responsible for the decline in bee populations. I've heard that this pesticide is not only used in bug sprays, but also in seemingly innocent products like flower bulbs, plant plugs and certain types of compost.  Is this true? If so, how do I make sure I don’t already have this stuff at home? How can I recognise products or brands that contain/use these pesticides? To recognize products with neonicotinoid pesticides, look for active ingredients such as imidacloprid, clothianidin, thiamethoxam, acetamiprid, thiacloprid, and dinotefuran on the product label. These chemicals are often found in bug sprays, but also in gardening products like flower bulbs, plant plugs, and certain composts. While the EU has temporarily banned many uses, some products or imported goods may still contain them. Always check the labels, ask retailers for neonicotinoid-free certifications, or choose products labeled as organic or bee-friendly
